meta.title: 4.7 Agentic Workflows in a Design Tool
meta.description: Understand what a canvas-aware agent requires — design token mapping, component library matching, canvas-to-code tool calls — and why the layer connecting Figma variables to generated code is a Platform PM deliverable, not emergent model behavior.

blocks.prompt:
> Most coding agents work in a terminal or IDE. Make's agent works inside Figma — a design tool. What capabilities does a canvas-aware agent need that a terminal agent doesn't?

Write your answer before reading.

---

blocks.content:
## What You'll Learn

By the end of this module, you'll be able to explain why a terminal coding agent and a canvas-aware agent have fundamentally different context requirements, identify which agent failures are platform tool failures versus model failures, and articulate why design token mapping is a Platform PM deliverable rather than emergent model behavior.

A canvas-aware coding agent requires a Figma-native tool layer — component mapping, design token resolution, and Figma API integration — that cannot be provided by any general-purpose coding agent, because the context that produces faithful, maintainable code lives in the design file, not in the model's training data. Its context is text: file contents, shell output, error messages, documentation. Make's agent lives in a design tool. Its context includes a visual artifact — a canvas with frames, components, layout rules, and design tokens. This changes the agent's requirements at every layer.

**Design-aware code generation:**

A terminal coding agent receives text instructions: "Create a login form with an email field and password field." It generates code based on its training data's understanding of what a login form looks like.

Make's agent receives something different: a Figma frame containing an already-designed login form, with specific layout (flex column, 24px gap), spacing (email field: 48px height, 16px padding), typography (Inter 14px regular for labels, Inter 16px for inputs), and color (border: `--color-border-subtle`, background: `--color-surface-primary`).

The agent must read these properties from the canvas and generate code that matches them — not code that resembles a generic login form. The output is a React component that uses the exact design tokens from the file's variable library, the exact component from the design system (if `Input` exists as a design system component, use it), and the exact layout properties from the frame.

This requires tools that do not exist in any terminal agent's toolkit.

**Component library integration:**

A Figma design system contains components: `Button`, `Input`, `Card`, `Badge`, `Modal`. When the agent generates code, it should use these components — not generate raw HTML elements that approximate them.

This requires a tool: given a UI element in the design frame, find the closest matching component in the codebase's component library. This is a matching problem: the Figma component named "Button/Primary/Medium" should map to `<Button variant="primary" size="md" />` in the React codebase.

Building this mapping layer is a Platform PM deliverable. It is not something the reasoning model does spontaneously — it requires a structured mapping between the design system's component taxonomy and the codebase's component API.

**Design token mapping:**

Figma variables (color styles, typography styles, spacing values) must map to the codebase's design tokens (CSS custom properties, Tailwind theme values, styled-components theme keys).

A frame with `fill: Brand/Primary/500` must generate code with `color: var(--color-brand-primary-500)` — not `color: #5B21B6` (the resolved hex value). Hard-coding resolved values defeats the purpose of a design token system.

The agent needs a tool that queries the Figma file's variable library and returns the token name, not just the resolved value. The platform must maintain this mapping as both the Figma file and the codebase evolve.

blocks.question: A designer updates the brand primary color from #5B21B6 to a new value in the Figma file. The Make agent generated code last week using `color: #5B21B6` (hard-coded hex). The agent generated code today using `color: var(--color-brand-primary-500)` (token reference). Which version survives the brand update without a code change, and why?
blocks.answer: The token reference version survives without a code change. When the Figma file's variable for `Brand/Primary/500` is updated to the new hex value, the CSS custom property `--color-brand-primary-500` resolves to the new value automatically — any component using the token reference updates without touching a single line of code. The hard-coded hex version requires finding every file where `#5B21B6` appears and updating it manually. In a large codebase generated by Make across many sessions, that could be hundreds of files.

blocks.content:
**Canvas-to-code bidirectional sync:**

The most ambitious version of Make's vision is bidirectional: code changes update the canvas, canvas changes update the code. A designer moves a button 8px to the right on the canvas; the agent updates the `margin-left` in the component. A developer changes `gap: 24px` to `gap: 16px` in code; the canvas updates to reflect the new spacing.

This is technically hard. It requires:
- Parsing code changes to extract layout properties
- Mapping those properties back to Figma frame attributes
- Writing changes to the Figma file via the Figma API
- Doing all of this without creating infinite loops (code change → canvas change → code change → ...)

The "Code to Canvas" initiative (noted in the JD as a partnership with Anthropic) is working in this direction. The Platform PM owns the protocol for this sync.

**Multi-modal context:**

Terminal agents receive text. Make's agent should receive both text and visual context. When a user says "this button doesn't match the design," the agent should be able to see a screenshot of the rendered button and a screenshot of the Figma frame, and compute the difference.

This requires vision capability (Claude's multi-modal input) and a screenshot tool — a tool that captures the current preview render and returns it as an image the model can analyze.

This is architecturally different from anything a terminal agent does. The model must reason across two modalities: the Figma visual spec and the rendered code output.

**The Figma API as a tool registry:**

The agent's Figma-specific tools call the Figma REST API:

- `GET /v1/files/:file_key/nodes?ids=:node_id` — read a specific frame's properties
- `GET /v1/files/:file_key/variables/local` — read the file's variable library
- `GET /v1/files/:file_key/components` — read the file's component set
- `POST /v1/files/:file_key/changes` — write changes back to the canvas

These API calls must be authenticated, rate-limited, and error-handled. They are tools in the agent's tool catalog — which means they are subject to all the permission, sandboxing, and reliability requirements from Module 4.2.

**Why this is Make's moat:**

No terminal coding agent has access to a design system at this level of integration. Cursor does not know that the button it just generated has 2px more padding than the design spec. Claude Code does not know that the component it created should be using the project's existing `Card` component rather than a raw `div`. GitHub Copilot does not know that the color it hard-coded is semantically a brand-primary color that should be a token.

Make's agent knows all of this — because the platform gives it Figma-native context. This is not a model capability advantage. It is a context advantage. And context is the Platform PM's domain.

---

## How to Think About This

The mental model is context ownership. A terminal agent's context is text the user provides. Make's agent's context is a structured artifact — a Figma file — that the platform must extract and deliver. When the agent uses a raw div instead of the project's PricingCard component, the instinct is to blame the model. The correct diagnosis is a platform failure: the component mapping tool didn't surface the match. Every time an agent output diverges from the design spec, ask first whether the platform delivered the right context before concluding the model failed.

blocks.question:
A user selects a Figma frame containing a pricing table and says "code this up." The frame uses a Figma component called "PricingCard" from the design system. The codebase has a React component called `<PricingCard />` in `src/components/ui/PricingCard.tsx`. The agent generates the code using raw `<div>` elements instead of `<PricingCard />`. What is the root cause?
A. The model is not smart enough to know about PricingCard
B. The component mapping tool did not surface the match between the Figma "PricingCard" component and the React `PricingCard` component
C. The agent should have read every file in the codebase to find PricingCard
D. The user should have told the agent to use PricingCard explicitly
blocks.answer:
B.** This is a platform failure, not a model failure and not a user failure. The agent generated reasonable code given the context it had — but the platform did not provide the component mapping that would have told it "this Figma component corresponds to this React component." The fix is in the tool layer (component mapping tool), not in the model weights or the user prompt.

---

blocks.title:
Open any Figma file you have access to. Pick one frame. Write out — in prose — the complete set of information the agent would need to generate a pixel-perfect code implementation: layout properties, spacing, typography, colors, component instances, interactive states. Then: which of these would require a custom Figma API tool call, and which could the agent infer from a screenshot alone?

---
blocks.solution: You should find that a screenshot captures visual output (approximate spacing, colors, typography, overall layout) but cannot reliably surface the underlying structure: whether a color is a hardcoded hex or a token reference, whether a component is an instance of a Figma component or a frame that looks like one, what the exact pixel values are versus what the visual approximation suggests, and what the responsive behavior or interactive states are. The Figma API tool calls are required for: resolving token names (not just resolved hex values), identifying component instances and their component keys, reading auto-layout constraints (not inferred from visual spacing), and reading variant properties. A screenshot-only approach would produce code that looks approximately correct visually but uses hard-coded values and raw divs instead of tokens and design system components — which means it breaks on rebrand and diverges from the design system over time.

blocks.prompt:
Explain to a Figma designer (non-technical) why the agent using design tokens instead of hard-coded hex values matters for their workflow. What breaks in six months if the agent always hard-codes values?

---

questions: Think of a content pipeline or data transformation workflow you have built or managed — one where a structured input (a template, a schema, a design spec) had to be faithfully converted to a different format. What retrieval and mapping design choices mattered most to get the output right? What broke when the input changed?

When you have worked on products that bridged two systems with different data models — a design tool and a codebase, a CMS and a front-end, an analytics system and a reporting layer — where did the mapping layer break down first? What does that tell you about the component mapping problem in Make?

---

blocks.content:
A canvas-aware coding agent requires a Figma-native tool layer — component mapping, design token resolution, Figma API integration — that is Make's proprietary moat and cannot be provided by any general-purpose coding agent.

---

blocks.prompt:
> Every Figma API call costs tokens. Every agent session costs money. At scale, the unit economics of Make's agent determine whether the business works. What does a heavy Make user actually cost to serve?

---

---

reviewQuestions.question: What is the primary reason Make's agent must use design token names (like var(--color-brand-primary-500)) rather than resolved hex values (like #5B21B6) when generating code?
options: Hex values are not valid CSS in modern browsers, so design tokens are required for compatibility
When the brand color is updated in the Figma file, token references update automatically throughout the codebase, while hard-coded hex values must be found and replaced manually
Design tokens reduce CSS file size because the variable name is shorter than a hex value
reviewQuestions.explanation: Design tokens are semantic references that remain correct when the design system evolves. When the Figma variable for Brand/Primary/500 is updated, every component using the token reference updates automatically. Hard-coded hex values create maintenance debt that compounds across a large AI-generated codebase.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly! The durability of semantic references across design system changes is the core reason tokens matter.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Hex values are valid CSS, and file size isn't the concern. The key reason is maintainability: a token reference like var(--color-brand-primary-500) automatically picks up design system changes, while every hard-coded #5B21B6 must be manually hunted down and updated on a rebrand.

reviewQuestions.question: When Make's agent generates raw div elements instead of using the project's existing PricingCard React component, what is the correct diagnosis?
options: The underlying model lacks training data about design systems and needs to be fine-tuned
The component mapping tool failed to surface the match between the Figma 'PricingCard' component and the React PricingCard component — a platform failure, not a model failure
The user should have explicitly instructed the agent to use the existing component
reviewQuestions.explanation: The agent generated reasonable code given the context it received. The platform failed to provide the component mapping that would have told it which Figma component corresponds to which React component. The fix belongs in the tool layer, not in model retraining or user instructions.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Right! Platform tool failure is the correct diagnosis — the model behaved correctly given what it knew; the platform didn't give it the mapping.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: This is not a model training gap or a user error. The model can only use components it knows about — and it can only know about them if the platform's component mapping tool surfaces the match. The fix is building that tool layer, not retraining the model or asking users to manually specify component names.

reviewQuestions.question: Which of the following Figma API endpoints would the agent call to read a file's design token variable library?
reviewQuestions.explanation: The module lists GET /v1/files/:file_key/variables/local as the endpoint for reading the file's variable library (design tokens). The /components endpoint retrieves the component set; the /changes endpoint writes updates back to the canvas.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Nice! /variables/local is the endpoint for the design token library — distinct from components and the write endpoint.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: GET /v1/files/:file_key/components retrieves the component set, and POST /v1/files/:file_key/changes writes canvas updates. Reading the design token variable library requires GET /v1/files/:file_key/variables/local, as listed in the module's Figma API tool registry.
